title: Self-Supervised Learning
description: Contrastive learning, pretext tasks, SimCLR, MoCo, BYOL, MAE, and self-supervised approaches for vision, NLP, and graph representation learning.
details: Self-Supervised Learning is a paradigm where a model learns representations from unlabeled data by solving a pretext task that does not require human labels. The learned representations can then be fine-tuned for downstream tasks with few labels. This file covers contrastive learning (InfoNCE loss, temperature scaling, SimCLR, MoCo, SwAV), non-contrastive methods (BYOL, SimSiam — no negative pairs), masked image modeling (MAE, Masked Autoencoders), vision transformers for SSL (DINO, iBOT), NLP SSL (ELECTRA, replaced token detection, permuted language modeling), graph SSL (Deep Graph Infomax, GraphCL), the theoretical connection between contrastive learning and mutual information maximization, augmentation engineering for various modalities, and linear probing vs fine-tuning evaluation protocols. SSL is one of the most important recent advances in ML and is now a standard FAANG interview topic.

sections.title: Contrastive Learning: SimCLR & MoCo
sections.description:
[Intuition] Intuition: Pull similar (positive) pairs together in embedding space, push dissimilar (negative) pairs apart. Positives are created via data augmentation of the same image. Negatives are all other images in the batch.

InfoNCE (NT-Xent) loss: L = -log[ exp(sim(zi, zj)/τ) / Σ exp(sim(zi, zk)/τ) ]. The numerator is the positive pair similarity. The denominator sums over all negatives plus the positive. This loss is equivalent to (N-1)-way softmax classification where the positive pair is the correct class.

SimCLR:

  • Framework: augment x → xi and xj, encode with ResNet, project with MLP (projection head), apply InfoNCE loss.
  • Key findings: (1) Stronger augmentation = better representations. (2) MLP projection head is critical (removing it drops accuracy 10%+). (3) Larger batch size helps (more negatives). (4) Normalize embeddings to unit sphere + temperature scaling stabilizes training.
  • Limitation: Needs large batch (4096+) for enough negatives. Memory-intensive.

MoCo (Momentum Contrast):

  • Queue-based: Maintain a queue of K negative embeddings from previous mini-batches. Decouples batch size from number of negatives. Can use K up to 65K+.
  • Momentum encoder: Update encoder K by exponential moving average of query encoder: θk ← m θk + (1-m) θq. Prevents representation collapse by keeping the queue embeddings consistent.
  • MoCo v3: Added ViT backbone, improved training stability with batch normalization removed from ViT.
sections.codeBlock:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F

# === InfoNCE (NT-Xent) Loss ===
class NTXentLoss(nn.Module):
    """Normalized Temperature-scaled Cross Entropy Loss."""
    def __init__(self, temperature=0.5):
        super().__init__()
        self.temperature = temperature
    
    def forward(self, z_i, z_j):
        """
        z_i, z_j: [B, D] embeddings from two augmented views
        Returns: scalar loss
        """
        B = z_i.size(0)
        
        # Normalize embeddings
        z_i = F.normalize(z_i, dim=1)
        z_j = F.normalize(z_j, dim=1)
        
        # Concatenate: [2B, D]
        z = torch.cat([z_i, z_j], dim=0)
        
        # Compute similarity matrix
        sim = torch.mm(z, z.T) / self.temperature  # [2B, 2B]
        
        # Mask out self-similarity (diagonal)
        mask = torch.eye(2 * B, device=z.device, dtype=torch.bool)
        sim = sim.masked_fill(mask, float('-inf'))
        
        # Positives: (i, i+B) and (i+B, i) are the two views of same image
        pos = torch.cat([
            torch.arange(B, 2 * B, device=z.device),
            torch.arange(0, B, device=z.device)
        ])  # indices of positive for each row
        
        # Cross-entropy: positive / sum of exp(all negatives + positive)
        loss = F.cross_entropy(sim, pos)
        return loss

# === SimCLR (simplified) ===
class SimCLR(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, backbone, projection_dim=128):
        super().__init__()
        self.backbone = backbone  # e.g., ResNet-50
        self.projection = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Linear(2048, 2048),
            nn.ReLU(),
            nn.Linear(2048, projection_dim),
        )
    
    def forward(self, x_i, x_j):
        """Two augmented views of the same image."""
        h_i = self.backbone(x_i)  # [B, 2048]
        h_j = self.backbone(x_j)
        
        z_i = self.projection(h_i)  # [B, proj_dim]
        z_j = self.projection(h_j)
        
        return z_i, z_j

# === MoCo v3 (simplified) ===
class MoCo(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, backbone, dim=128, K=65536, m=0.999):
        """
        K: queue size (number of negatives)
        m: momentum coefficient
        """
        super().__init__()
        self.K = K
        self.m = m
        
        # Query encoder
        self.encoder_q = backbone
        self.proj_q = nn.Linear(2048, dim)
        
        # Momentum encoder (EMA of query)
        self.encoder_k = backbone
        self.proj_k = nn.Linear(2048, dim)
        
        # Copy weights, disable gradients
        for param_q, param_k in zip(self.encoder_q.parameters(),
                                     self.encoder_k.parameters()):
            param_k.data.copy_(param_q.data)
            param_k.requires_grad = False
        for param_q, param_k in zip(self.proj_q.parameters(),
                                     self.proj_k.parameters()):
            param_k.data.copy_(param_q.data)
            param_k.requires_grad = False
        
        # Queue of negative keys
        self.register_buffer('queue', torch.randn(dim, K))
        self.queue = F.normalize(self.queue, dim=0)
        self.register_buffer('queue_ptr', torch.zeros(1, dtype=torch.long))
    
    @torch.no_grad()
    def _momentum_update(self):
        for param_q, param_k in zip(self.encoder_q.parameters(),
                                     self.encoder_k.parameters()):
            param_k.data = self.m * param_k.data + (1 - self.m) * param_q.data
        for param_q, param_k in zip(self.proj_q.parameters(),
                                     self.proj_k.parameters()):
            param_k.data = self.m * param_k.data + (1 - self.m) * param_q.data
    
    @torch.no_grad()
    def _dequeue_and_enqueue(self, keys):
        # Enqueue current batch keys, dequeue oldest
        B = keys.size(0)
        ptr = int(self.queue_ptr)
        self.queue[:, ptr:ptr + B] = keys.T
        self.queue_ptr[0] = (ptr + B) % self.K
    
    def forward(self, im_q, im_k):
        q = F.normalize(self.proj_q(self.encoder_q(im_q)), dim=1)
        
        with torch.no_grad():
            self._momentum_update()
            k = F.normalize(self.proj_k(self.encoder_k(im_k)), dim=1)
        
        # Positive logits: [B, 1]
        l_pos = torch.einsum('nc,nc->n', q, k).unsqueeze(-1)
        # Negative logits: [B, K]
        l_neg = torch.einsum('nc,ck->nk', q, self.queue.clone().detach())
        
        # Logits: [B, 1 + K]
        logits = torch.cat([l_pos, l_neg], dim=1) / 0.07
        labels = torch.zeros(logits.size(0), dtype=torch.long, device=q.device)
        
        loss = F.cross_entropy(logits, labels)
        
        self._dequeue_and_enqueue(k)
        return loss

sections.title: Non-Contrastive Methods: BYOL & MAE
sections.description:
[Intuition] Intuition: Contrastive methods need negative pairs (which requires large batches or queues). BYOL & SimSiam avoid negatives entirely — they predict one view's representation from another view, using a stop-gradient or momentum to prevent collapse. MAE masks out patches and reconstructs them (like a BERT for images).

BYOL (Bootstrap Your Own Latent):

  • Online network: encode view1, predict target's projection.
  • Target network: encode view2 (momentum-updated), no gradient.
  • Loss: MSE between predicted and target projections (L2).
  • Why doesn't it collapse? The momentum encoder and prediction MLP create an asymmetric path. The target representation lags behind the online representation, preventing the trivial solution (all zeros). BYOL works even with BN but can collapse without it — analysis shows BN implicitly centers representations.

SimSiam:

  • Same architecture as BYOL but NO momentum encoder. Uses stop-gradient on one branch, prediction MLP on the other.
  • Stop-gradient + predictor = implicit EM algorithm: one step of k-means clustering on representations.

MAE (Masked Autoencoders):

  • Mask random patches of the image (75% mask ratio). Encode visible patches only with ViT. Decode full set of patches (visible + masked tokens) with lightweight decoder. Loss: MSE between reconstructed and original pixels for masked patches only.
  • Design choices: (1) High masking ratio (75%) is critical — forces meaningful reconstruction. (2) Asymmetric encoder-decoder: encoder only sees visible patches (efficient), decoder is lightweight. (3) Decoder can be shallow (4-8 Transformer blocks).
  • Transfer performance improves with model size and compute — scales better than contrastive methods for very large models.
sections.codeBlock:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn

# === BYOL ===
class BYOL(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, backbone, proj_dim=256, hidden_dim=4096, m=0.996):
        super().__init__()
        self.m = m
        
        # Online network
        self.online_encoder = backbone
        self.online_proj = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Linear(2048, hidden_dim), nn.BatchNorm1d(hidden_dim), nn.ReLU(),
            nn.Linear(hidden_dim, proj_dim)
        )
        self.predictor = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Linear(proj_dim, hidden_dim), nn.BatchNorm1d(hidden_dim), nn.ReLU(),
            nn.Linear(hidden_dim, proj_dim)
        )
        
        # Target network (EMA of online)
        self.target_encoder = backbone
        self.target_proj = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Linear(2048, hidden_dim), nn.BatchNorm1d(hidden_dim), nn.ReLU(),
            nn.Linear(hidden_dim, proj_dim)
        )
        
        # Initialize target with online weights
        self._init_target()
        for p in self.target_encoder.parameters():
            p.requires_grad = False
        for p in self.target_proj.parameters():
            p.requires_grad = False
    
    def _init_target(self):
        for p_online, p_target in zip(self.online_encoder.parameters(),
                                        self.target_encoder.parameters()):
            p_target.data.copy_(p_online.data)
        for p_online, p_target in zip(self.online_proj.parameters(),
                                        self.target_proj.parameters()):
            p_target.data.copy_(p_online.data)
    
    @torch.no_grad()
    def _momentum_update(self):
        for p_online, p_target in zip(self.online_encoder.parameters(),
                                        self.target_encoder.parameters()):
            p_target.data = self.m * p_target.data + (1 - self.m) * p_online.data
        for p_online, p_target in zip(self.online_proj.parameters(),
                                        self.target_proj.parameters()):
            p_target.data = self.m * p_target.data + (1 - self.m) * p_online.data
    
    def forward(self, x1, x2):
        # Online: predict target representation
        z1 = self.online_proj(self.online_encoder(x1))
        p1 = self.predictor(z1)
        
        with torch.no_grad():
            self._momentum_update()
            z2 = self.target_proj(self.target_encoder(x2))
            z2 = F.normalize(z2, dim=1)
        
        p1 = F.normalize(p1, dim=1)
        loss = 2 - 2 * (p1 * z2).sum(dim=1)  # MSE = 2 - 2*cos_sim
        return loss.mean()

# === SimSiam (even simpler: no momentum) ===
class SimSiam(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, backbone, proj_dim=2048, pred_dim=512):
        super().__init__()
        self.encoder = backbone
        self.proj = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Linear(2048, proj_dim, bias=False),
            nn.BatchNorm1d(proj_dim),
            nn.ReLU(),
            nn.Linear(proj_dim, proj_dim, bias=False),
            nn.BatchNorm1d(proj_dim),
            nn.ReLU(),
            nn.Linear(proj_dim, proj_dim),
            nn.BatchNorm1d(proj_dim),
        )
        self.predictor = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Linear(proj_dim, pred_dim, bias=False),
            nn.BatchNorm1d(pred_dim),
            nn.ReLU(),
            nn.Linear(pred_dim, proj_dim),
        )
    
    def forward(self, x1, x2):
        z1 = self.proj(self.encoder(x1))
        z2 = self.proj(self.encoder(x2))
        
        p1, p2 = self.predictor(z1), self.predictor(z2)
        
        z1 = z1.detach()  # stop gradient!
        z2 = z2.detach()
        
        loss = F.mse_loss(p1, z2) / 2 + F.mse_loss(p2, z1) / 2
        return loss

# === MAE (Masked Autoencoder) ===
def random_masking(x, mask_ratio=0.75):
    """x: [B, N, D] patches. Returns masked_x, mask, ids_restore."""
    B, N, D = x.shape
    len_keep = int(N * (1 - mask_ratio))
    
    noise = torch.rand(B, N, device=x.device)
    ids_shuffle = torch.argsort(noise, dim=1)
    ids_restore = torch.argsort(ids_shuffle, dim=1)
    
    ids_keep = ids_shuffle[:, :len_keep]
    x_masked = torch.gather(x, dim=1, index=ids_keep.unsqueeze(-1).repeat(1, 1, D))
    
    mask = torch.ones([B, N], device=x.device)
    mask[:, :len_keep] = 0
    mask = torch.gather(mask, dim=1, index=ids_restore)
    
    return x_masked, mask, ids_restore

sections.title: SSL for NLP & Graphs
sections.description:
[Intuition] Intuition: The same principles apply across modalities: predict parts from other parts. For text: predict masked tokens, replaced tokens, or sentence order. For graphs: predict subgraph structure or contrast node-graph pairs.

NLP SSL methods:

  • Masked Language Modeling (MLM): Mask 15% of tokens, predict them. Used by BERT (devlin et al.). Simple, effective, but requires bidirectional attention (no autoregressive generation).
  • ELECTRA (Replaced Token Detection): Small generator network replaces some tokens with plausible fakes. Discriminator predicts which tokens were replaced. More efficient than MLM because the model learns from ALL tokens, not just masked ones (15% vs 100%).
  • Permuted Language Modeling (XLNet): Factorize sequence probability in all possible orders. Combines the benefits of autoregressive (left-to-right) with bidirectional context.
  • Sentence-level objectives: Next Sentence Prediction (NSP), Sentence Order Prediction (SOP). Used in ALBERT, RoBERTa.

SSL for graphs:

  • Deep Graph Infomax: Maximize mutual information between node representations and a pooled graph representation. Uses a discriminator to distinguish positive (node, graph) pairs from negative (corrupted node, graph) pairs.
  • GraphCL: Contrastive learning on graphs via graph augmentations: node dropping, edge perturbation, attribute masking, subgraph sampling.
  • GPT-GNN: Autoregressive generation of graph: predict masked edges and masked node attributes. Pretrain GNN via generation, then fine-tune.
sections.codeBlock:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F

# === ELECTRA: Replaced Token Detection ===
# Generator (small MLM) + Discriminator (replaced token classifier)

class ELECTRALoss(nn.Module):
    """
    Generator: masked LM loss on 15% tokens.
    Discriminator: binary classification on ALL tokens (replaced or not).
    The generator loss also backpropagates through generator.
    """
    def __init__(self, generator, discriminator):
        super().__init__()
        self.generator = generator
        self.discriminator = discriminator
    
    def forward(self, input_ids, attention_mask, masked_positions, labels):
        # Generator: predict masked tokens
        gen_logits = self.generator(input_ids, attention_mask)
        gen_loss = F.cross_entropy(
            gen_logits[masked_positions],
            labels[masked_positions]
        )
        
        # Sample replacements from generator
        with torch.no_grad():
            gen_probs = F.softmax(gen_logits, dim=-1)
            sampled_tokens = torch.multinomial(
                gen_probs.view(-1, gen_probs.size(-1)), 1
            ).view(gen_probs.size(0), -1)
        
        # Create corrupted input
        corrupted = input_ids.clone()
        corrupted[masked_positions] = sampled_tokens[masked_positions]
        
        # Discriminator: predict which tokens were replaced
        disc_logits = self.discriminator(corrupted, attention_mask)
        disc_labels = torch.zeros_like(input_ids)
        disc_labels[masked_positions] = (sampled_tokens[masked_positions]
                                          != labels[masked_positions]).float()
        
        disc_loss = F.binary_cross_entropy_with_logits(
            disc_logits, disc_labels
        )
        
        return gen_loss + disc_loss

# === Deep Graph Infomax (DGI) ===
class DGI(nn.Module):
    """Maximize MI between node and graph representations."""
    def __init__(self, encoder, hidden_dim):
        super().__init__()
        self.encoder = encoder  # GNN
        self.discriminator = nn.Bilinear(hidden_dim, hidden_dim, 1)
        self.readout = lambda h: torch.sigmoid(h.mean(dim=0))  # graph summary
    
    def forward(self, x, edge_index):
        # Positive: original graph
        pos_z = self.encoder(x, edge_index)  # [N, D]
        pos_summary = self.readout(pos_z)  # [D]
        
        # Negative: corrupted graph (shuffle node features)
        perm = torch.randperm(x.size(0))
        neg_x = x[perm]
        neg_z = self.encoder(neg_x, edge_index)
        
        # Discriminate (node, graph) pairs
        pos_out = self.discriminator(pos_z, pos_summary.expand_as(pos_z))
        neg_out = self.discriminator(neg_z, pos_summary.expand_as(neg_z))
        
        # Binary cross-entropy loss
        pos_loss = F.binary_cross_entropy_with_logits(
            pos_out, torch.ones_like(pos_out)
        )
        neg_loss = F.binary_cross_entropy_with_logits(
            neg_out, torch.zeros_like(neg_out)
        )
        return pos_loss + neg_loss

# === Evaluation: Linear probing vs Fine-tuning ===
# Linear probing: freeze backbone, train linear classifier
# Fine-tuning: update all parameters
# SSL eval protocol: linear probing first (tests representation quality)
#                   then fine-tuning (tests adaptability)
